title: TPU v1 · Profiling Data-Center Inference with a Roofline Model
oneLine: Before architecture work begins, profile Google's data-center inference workloads: reduce MLPs, CNNs, and LSTMs to matrix multiplication, then use a roofline model to distinguish compute-bound layers from bandwidth-bound ones—the core deliverable of workload analysis.

roof</text><text class="cs-note" x="100" y="40">Peak: 92 TOPS; many real layers remain on the slope</text></svg>

legend.name: Flat roof = compute bound
legend.desc: The horizontal ceiling is set by peak compute: TPU v1's 256×256 array contains 65,536 MACs at 700 MHz, yielding about 92 INT8 TOPS. Layers with sufficient arithmetic intensity approach this ceiling.

legend.name: Sloped roof = bandwidth bound
legend.desc: The slope is set by off-chip memory bandwidth: TPU v1's 8 GiB DDR3 delivers about 30 GB/s. Low-intensity layers cannot fetch operands fast enough, leaving compute idle on this slope.

legend.name: Ridge point
legend.desc: The intersection of the bandwidth and compute roofs equals peak compute divided by bandwidth. It is the minimum work per byte required to sustain peak compute.

legend.name: Memory-bound layer (A)
legend.desc: Small-batch layers with large weights—typically LSTMs and fully connected layers—reuse each fetched weight only once or twice. Their low arithmetic intensity places them left of the ridge, a common measured TPU bottleneck.

legend.name: Compute-bound layer (C)
legend.desc: Large matrices and large batches reuse weights many times. Their high arithmetic intensity places them right of the ridge, where they can exploit the systolic array's peak throughput.

steps.cap: ① Draw the roofs: peak compute caps the flat segment and off-chip bandwidth caps the slope; no workload can exceed either limit.
② Locate the ridge: compute ÷ bandwidth marks the arithmetic intensity needed to keep the array fully fed.
③ Plot workloads: large matrices and large batches (C) sit right of the ridge and approach the compute roof—the ideal case.
④ Key finding: many real layers (A: small batch/large weights; B: medium convolution) sit left of the ridge, bandwidth-bound and far below 92 TOPS.
⑤ Conclusion: inference is often memory-bound. The architecture needs both abundant compute and higher effective bandwidth through a large on-chip buffer and weight-stationary reuse.

before: Define the question first: What workloads must this chip serve? TPU targets Google's highest-volume data-center inference—MLPs, CNNs, and LSTMs—not training or one specific model.

Profile inputs to collect: Operator mix, matrix dimensions and batch sizes, operations versus memory traffic (arithmetic intensity), weight/activation working-set sizes, numerical-precision tolerance, and latency SLA.

Required evidence: Production traces or a representative model suite, compute density and off-chip bandwidth available in the candidate process, and numerical-format support in the TensorFlow/XLA ecosystem.

Hard constraints: Data-center power and latency limits, especially tail latency for online inference; the PCIe accelerator-card form factor; and continuing workload evolution. The profile cannot overfit one model.
during: Choose a common abstraction: MLPs, CNNs, and LSTMs can all be lowered to matrix multiplication (GEMM), so characterize the portfolio by matrix-multiply share and arithmetic intensity instead of optimizing model by model.

Measure precision tolerance: Inference tolerates low precision in practice, motivating INT8 multiplication with INT32 accumulation. Versus floating point, this cuts storage and bandwidth by about 4× while making each MAC smaller and more efficient.

Use a roofline to expose bottlenecks: Plot each layer by arithmetic intensity. Despite a high peak, many small-batch or weight-heavy layers lie on the bandwidth slope and are memory-bound—the same pattern shown in the TPU paper.

Balance compute and bandwidth: Adding MACs alone creates paper performance when layers are bandwidth-bound. Raise effective bandwidth as well: buffer activations in a large Unified Buffer, maximize weight reuse with a weight-stationary dataflow, and move the ridge left.

Balance breadth and specialization: Fully utilize compute on common large-matrix, large-batch work while supporting small-batch, low-latency cases. TPU favors throughput inference, dedicates most area to matrix multiplication, and accepts lower small-batch utilization.

Leave room for evolution: Workloads change with new models and longer contexts. Record trends in arithmetic intensity and bandwidth shortfall to guide later designs; this is sound engineering practice, not a disclosed TPU v1 roadmap.

result.label: Common abstraction
result.value: Lower all workloads to matrix multiplication (GEMM)

result.label: Precision decision
result.value: INT8 multiply / INT32 accumulate is sufficient

result.label: Key finding
result.value: Many layers are memory-bound, not compute-bound

result.label: Peak vs. measured
result.value: ~92 TOPS on paper; small-batch layers fall far short

result.label: Architecture implication
result.value: Pair abundant compute with effective bandwidth (large buffer / weight reuse)

after: Feed the profile into the compute core (§3.3): Matrix multiplication dominates, so implement a systolic array; INT8 tolerance fixes 8-bit MACs with 32-bit accumulation.

Feed the bandwidth gap into memory hierarchy (§3.4): The roofline exposes bandwidth as a bottleneck, requiring a large 24 MiB on-chip Unified Buffer for activations and weight-stationary reuse to raise arithmetic intensity and move the ridge left.
Define validation benchmarks: Use this representative workload suite plus roofline and cycle-level models as the scorecard for every later tradeoff, checking array utilization and end-to-end latency.

State uncertainty honestly: Refresh the profile as models evolve. TPU v1 uses off-chip DDR3; later designs would need more off-chip bandwidth to ease memory-bound behavior. That is a general inference-ASIC direction, not a disclosed v1 detail.
Close the loop with product definition (①/②): Confirm that throughput inference—with lower utilization at small batch sizes—matches the intended product position.
source: In-Datacenter Performance Analysis of a TPU, Jouppi et al., ISCA 2017. Facts disclosed in the paper are distinguished from illustrative examples and standard industry practice; the latter are not presented as disclosed TPU v1 implementation details.
